records.ety: [OE. seisen, saisen, OF. seisir, saisir, F. saisir, of Teutonic origin, and akin to E. set. The meaning is properly, to set, put, place, hence, to put in possession of. See Set, v. t.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Seized (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Seizing.]

defs: To fall or rush upon suddenly and lay hold of; to gripe or grasp suddenly; to reach and grasp.
quotes: For by no means the high bank he could seize.
Seek you to seize and gripe into your hands The royalties and rights of banished Hereford?

defs: To take possession of by force.
quotes: At last they seize The scepter, and regard not David's sons.

defs: To invade suddenly; to take sudden hold of; to come upon suddenly; as, a fever seizes a patient.
quotes: Hope and deubt alternate seize her seul.

defs: To take possession of by virtue of a warrant or other legal authority; as, the sheriff seized the debtor's goods.

quotes: As when a bear hath seized her cruel claws Upon the carcass of some beast too weak.

defs: To grap with the mind; to comprehend fully and distinctly; as, to seize an idea.
To bind or fasten together with a lashing of small stuff, as yarn or marline; as, to seize ropes.
notes: ☞ This word, by writers on law, is commonly written seise, in the phrase to be seised of (an estate), as also, in composition, disseise, disseisin.
